abstract: This paper explores the educational philosophy of Imam Al-Ghazali, specifically as articulated in his seminal work, *Ihya’ Ulumiddin*, and examines its enduring relevance in the contemporary pedagogical landscape. A towering figure in the Islamic tradition, Al-Ghazali argued that education should transcend the simple transfer of information, serving instead as a transformative process for shaping an individual’s character, ethics, and spiritual depth. Utilizing a library research methodology, this study analyzes Al-Ghazali’s core principles regarding moral and ethical development to evaluate how these traditional views can address the complex challenges of modern schooling. Al-Ghazali championed learning methods that are active, reflective, and anchored in spiritual values—models that offer a robust framework for strengthening character education today. Ultimately, this research seeks to bridge the gap between classical Islamic thought and modern educational needs, identifying how Al-Ghazali's holistic vision can contribute to a more balanced and ethically grounded global education system.
